2	ddx:
3	  id: ADR-004
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-004
6	    - FEAT-006
7	    - FEAT-010
8	---
9	# ADR-004: Use Bead-Backed Collections for DDx Runtime Storage
10	
11	## Status
12	
13	Accepted
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	DDx now has multiple local storage needs beyond the primary work queue:
18	
19	- active work items
20	- archived work items
21	- execution definitions and run indexes
22	- agent session indexes
23	- future runtime record families
24	
25	The current bead implementation already provides useful properties:
26	
27	- a portable bead schema with unknown-field preservation
28	- interchangeable local backends (`jsonl`, `bd`, `br`)
29	- locking and atomic rewrite behavior
30	
31	The current implementation is also too tightly coupled to one specific file,
32	`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, and to one primary use case, the active tracker.
33	
34	At the same time, execution runs and agent sessions can carry large payloads
35	such as prompts, responses, raw logs, and structured result blobs. Those
36	payloads should not be forced inline into one shared JSONL record.
37	
38	This decision must stay aligned with the bd/br interchange contract rather than
39	silently drifting into a DDx-only fork.
40	
41	Local compatibility anchors:
42	
43	- [types.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/types.go)
44	- [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go)
45	- [backend_external.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/backend_external.go)
46	- [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
47	- [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json)
48	
49	Upstream reference points:
50	
51	- `bd` upstream repository: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads>
52	- upstream format directory: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/tree/main/format>
53	- upstream Dolt storage notes: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/blob/main/docs/DOLT.md>
54	
55	DDx's external backend adapter currently treats both `bd` and `br` through the
56	same interchange surface:
57	
58	- `list --json` for reads
59	- `import --from jsonl --replace -` for writes
60	
61	That contract is the minimum compatibility surface this ADR must preserve.
62	
63	## Decision
64	
65	DDx will use the bead schema and backend model as the logical storage engine
66	for runtime records, but it will separate logical collections from one
67	specific physical file.
68	
69	1. DDx storage is organized as **named bead-backed collections**.
70	2. The primary tracker is one collection, not the only collection.
71	3. JSONL maps a collection to a separate file under `.ddx/`.
72	4. Other backends (`bd`, `br`) must preserve the same logical collection
73	   boundary, but their physical storage layout is a backend concern rather than
74	   a DDx file-layout concern.
75	5. Large payloads are stored in **sidecar attachment files** referenced by the
76	   bead-schema record, not inline in the collection row.
77	6. The base bead envelope must retain bd/br-compatible field names and
78	   semantics for the shared fields DDx already interoperates on.
79	
80	## Consequences
81	
82	### Positive
83	
84	- One reusable schema and backend family can serve work items, archives,
85	  execution indexes, and agent-session indexes.
86	- DDx can add new record families without inventing a new local storage model
87	  each time.
88	- Archived beads can move out of the active queue without changing the storage
89	  engine.
90	- Execution and agent records can keep lightweight searchable metadata in the
91	  bead collection while storing large bodies separately.
92	
93	### Negative
94	
95	- The current bead store API must be generalized from a fixed `beads.jsonl`
96	  target to a named collection abstraction.
97	- Collection-specific semantics must be documented clearly because not every
98	  bead-backed collection uses queue semantics such as `ready` or `blocked`.
99	- DDx must not assume that every backend exposes collections as separate files.
100	
101	## Collection Model
102	
103	Examples of logical collections:
104	
105	- `beads` — active work queue
106	- `beads-archive` — archived work items
107	- `exec-runs` — execution run metadata records
108	- `agent-sessions` — agent session metadata records
109	
110	Each record still uses the bead envelope:
111	
112	- `id`
113	- `issue_type`
114	- `status`
115	- `title`
116	- `labels`
117	- `created_at`
118	- `updated_at`
119	- preserved unknown fields for domain-specific data
120	
121	Shared field names remain aligned with bd/br interchange:
122	
123	- `issue_type`, not `type`
124	- `owner`, not `assignee`
125	- `created_at` / `updated_at`, not `created` / `updated`
126	- `dependencies`, not `deps`
127	
128	Collection-specific fields live in preserved extras. For example:
129	
130	- execution runs may store `definition_id`, `artifact_ids`, `run_status`,
131	  `provenance`, and attachment references
132	- agent sessions may store `harness`, `model`, `tokens`, `correlation`, and
133	  attachment references
134	
135	## Attachment Model
136	
137	Large bodies are stored outside the collection row.
138	
139	Examples:
140	
141	- prompt text
142	- response text
143	- stdout or stderr logs
144	- structured result JSON
145	
146	The bead-backed metadata record stores stable references to these payloads.
147	
148	Attachment references are DDx-specific extensions carried in preserved extras.
149	They must not rename or redefine the shared bead-envelope fields that bd/br
150	already understand.
151	
152	## Technical Guards
153	
154	This ADR is only acceptable if the compatibility contract is enforced in-repo.
155	
156	Required guards:
157	
158	1. The base bead envelope is defined in
159	   [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json).
160	2. Unknown fields remain allowed at the schema level so bd/br computed fields
161	   and DDx-specific extensions both round-trip.
162	3. [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
163	   must keep locking the field names and round-trip behavior against real bd
164	   export examples.
165	4. [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go) remains
166	   the canonical field-mapping implementation for DDx bead JSON.
167	5. Collection-specific runtime records may add their own schemas, but those
168	   schemas must layer on top of the base bead record rather than fork it.
169	
170	If upstream bd/br changes the shared JSON field contract, DDx must update the
171	schema file, compatibility tests, and this ADR together.
172	
173	## Not Chosen
174	
175	### Separate bespoke storage formats per subsystem
176	
177	Rejected because it multiplies local persistence models and weakens backend
178	reuse.
179	
180	### Storing all execution and session payloads inline in collection rows
181	
182	Rejected because prompts, responses, and logs can grow large and would make
183	shared collection rewrites fragile and expensive.
184	
185	### Reusing only bead locking code while inventing a new record schema
186	
187	Rejected because the bead schema is already the portable DDx record envelope
188	and should remain the common logical model.
189

8	---
9	# Technical Design: Artifact Graph Schema
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design defines the graph record shape used after artifact identity expands
14	beyond markdown documents. It complements TD-025, which defines how artifact
15	identity is discovered from embedded frontmatter and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files.
16	
17	The graph must expose `media_type`, represent `generated_by` as a provenance
18	edge, and keep generated-artifact staleness separate from ordinary dependency
19	staleness.
20	
21	## Graph Nodes
22	
23	Each discovered artifact produces one graph node:
24	
25	| Field | Type | Description |
26	|-------|------|-------------|
27	| `id` | string | Stable artifact id from `ddx.id`. |
28	| `path` | string | Repository-relative path to the artifact bytes or markdown file. |
29	| `identity_source` | enum | `frontmatter` for markdown, `sidecar` for non-markdown media. |
30	| `sidecar_path` | string? | Repository-relative sidecar path when `identity_source=sidecar`. |
31	| `media_type` | string | IANA-style media type. Markdown defaults to `text/markdown`; sidecars should set it explicitly and may be inferred only as a compatibility fallback. |
32	| `hash` | string | Current content hash. Markdown excludes the managed `review` block; sidecar artifacts hash paired artifact bytes. |
33	| `review` | object? | Last reviewed hash/dependency map written by `ddx doc stamp`. |
34	| `generated_by` | object? | `{ run, prompt, source_hash }` provenance copied from identity metadata. |
35	
36	Graph APIs must preserve unknown identity fields for detail views, but graph
37	construction only interprets the fields above plus `depends_on`.
38	
39	## Edge Types
40	
41	The graph has two first-class edge types:
42	
43	| Edge type | Source | Target | Authority? | Staleness behavior |
44	|-----------|--------|--------|------------|--------------------|
45	| `depends_on` | Artifact id | Upstream artifact id | Yes | Participates in review hash comparison and cascade propagation. |
46	| `generated_by` | Artifact id | Run id | No | Provenance only. Never cascades to dependents. |
47	
48	`depends_on` edges are artifact-to-artifact edges. `generated_by` edges are
49	artifact-to-run edges that point into the unified FEAT-010 run substrate. A run
50	record that produced an artifact should also carry `produces_artifact` so the

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: SD-004
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-004
6	    - ADR-001
7	    - ADR-003
8	---
9	# Solution Design: Beads Tracker
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	This design specifies the runtime behavior of `ddx bead` and the file-backed
14	tracker used by HELIX and other workflows. The key design constraint is that
15	the default active-work collection must remain safe under concurrent access and
16	recoverable after partial corruption without requiring a separate database.
17	
18	## Goals
19	
20	- Keep the default backend human-readable and portable.
21	- Preserve unknown fields end-to-end for workflow-specific metadata.
22	- Make queue operations deterministic from a single parsed snapshot.
23	- Prevent corruption from concurrent writers.
24	- Self-heal partially corrupted JSONL when valid beads still exist.
25	- Expose enough context to diagnose malformed input without losing the whole queue.
26	
27	## Reuse Boundary
28	
29	The bead store is the reusable DDx record-store substrate, but the current
30	implementation is too tightly coupled to the primary tracker file.
31	
32	- The backend interface is bead-shaped, which is useful: it gives DDx one
33	  portable record schema with unknown-field preservation and interchangeable
34	  backends.
35	- The missing boundary is between the storage engine and one specific file such
36	  as `beads.jsonl`.
37	- DDx should support multiple named bead-backed collections, each with its own
38	  retention and indexing behavior.
39	
40	The reusable pieces are lower-level:
41	
42	- directory locking
43	- atomic temp-file swap
44	- repo-local backend selection conventions
45	- best-effort repair behavior where a line-oriented store is appropriate
46	- a stable bead-schema envelope with domain-specific fields in preserved extras
47	
48	Execution, metric, archive, and agent-session storage should reuse those
49	mechanics through separate bead-backed collections rather than sharing the
50	primary active-work file.
51	
52	## Data Format
53	
54	### Primary File
55	
56	- Path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl` for the default active-work collection
57	- Format: one JSON object per line
58	- Ordering: written sorted by `id`
59	- Semantics: each line is one full bead record; blank lines are ignored
60	
61	### Repair Artifacts
62	
63	- Backup path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak`
64	- Temporary write path: `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`
65	- Backups are created only when the store auto-repairs a partially corrupted file
66	- The replacement write is atomic on the same filesystem via `rename`
67	
68	### Field Preservation
69	
70	Known fields are parsed into the bead struct. Unknown fields are preserved in
71	`Extra` and round-trip through read/write and import/export flows. This is the
72	mechanism that allows HELIX to store fields such as `spec-id`,
73	`execution-eligible`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`, `superseded-by`, and
74	`replaces`.
75	
76	The design also reserves two workflow-facing shapes:
77	
78	- `assignee` is the advisory owner recorded by claim operations.
79	- `events` is an append-only array of execution evidence records stored in
80	  `Extra["events"]`.
81	
82	Each evidence record carries `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`,
83	`created_at`, and `source`. DDx treats the array as ordered history and never
84	rewrites or compacts it during normal operations.
85	
86	## Claim Algorithm
87	
88	Claim/unclaim stays a normal bead mutation, but ownership is now explicit.
89	
90	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
91	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
92	3. Resolve the assignee from the explicit `--assignee` flag, then runtime
93	   caller identity, then `ddx` as the fallback.
94	4. For `--claim`, set `status=in_progress`, `assignee`, `claimed-at`, and
95	   `claimed-pid`.
96	5. For `--unclaim`, set `status=open` and clear `assignee`, `claimed-at`, and
97	   `claimed-pid`.
98	6. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
99	7. Atomically rename the temp file into place.
100	
101	Claims remain advisory; the store does not introduce a hard reservation lock.
102	The metadata exists so humans and agents can distinguish who holds the claim
103	and when it happened.
104	
105	## Evidence Algorithm
106	
107	Execution evidence is append-only.
108	
109	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
110	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
111	3. Load the target bead and append one evidence entry to `Extra["events"]`.
112	4. Populate `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`, `created_at`, and `source`.
113	5. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
114	6. Atomically rename the temp file into place.
115	
116	This deliberately preserves full history. Evidence writes never mutate or
117	remove existing entries, so close summaries and experiment outcomes remain
118	auditable.
119	
120	## Read Path
121	
122	The read path is intentionally best-effort.
123	
124	1. Read the full file from disk.
125	2. Scan line-by-line instead of parsing the entire file as one JSON value.
126	3. Trim whitespace per line.
127	4. Unmarshal each non-empty line independently.
128	5. Preserve valid beads in a snapshot.
129	6. Emit line-numbered warnings for malformed records.
130	7. Return `events` history and claim metadata exactly as stored.
131	8. If at least one valid bead exists and at least one malformed record was seen,
132	   trigger repair under the store lock.
133	9. If no valid bead exists, return a contextual error that names the file and
134	   malformed-record count.
135	
136	Why this shape:
137	
138	- A single malformed record should not break `ready`, `blocked`, `status`, or
139	  `list`.
140	- Line numbers are enough to isolate the bad record quickly.
141	- A full snapshot keeps queue views deterministic within a single command.
142	
143	## Repair Path
144	
145	Repair only runs when the file contains a mix of valid and malformed records.
146	
147	1. Acquire the bead store lock.
148	2. Re-read the current file under the lock.
149	3. Reparse it to verify it still needs repair.
150	4. Copy the current file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak` using a temp file plus rename.
151	5. Rewrite the cleaned bead snapshot using the normal atomic writer.
152	6. Leave the backup in place for inspection and rollback.
153	
154	This prevents concurrent readers from racing to repair the same file after it
155	has already been fixed by another process.
156	
157	## Write Path
158	
159	Mutating commands use the same pattern:
160	
161	1. Acquire the store lock.
162	2. Read the current bead snapshot.
163	3. Apply the mutation in memory.
164	4. Validate the result.
165	5. Serialize the full snapshot to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.tmp`.
166	6. Rename the temp file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
167	
168	This ensures there is never a partially written main tracker file. The lock
169	serializes writers, and the temp-file swap prevents readers from seeing a half
170	written JSONL record.
171	
172	## Queue Derivation
173	
174	The tracker queue views are derived from one in-memory snapshot.
175	
176	### Ready
177	
178	- Consider only `open` beads.
179	- A bead is ready when every dependency is `closed`.
180	- Execution-ready views additionally filter on `execution-eligible` and
181	  `superseded-by`.
182	- Results are sorted by priority, then by stable iteration order from the
183	  parsed snapshot.
184	
185	### Blocked
186	
187	- Consider only `open` beads.
188	- A bead is blocked when at least one dependency is not `closed`.
189	
190	### Status
191	
192	- `Total` is the number of parsed beads.
193	- `Open`, `Closed`, `Ready`, and `Blocked` are derived from the same snapshot.
194	- Status reporting never reparses the file independently of the queue view.
195	- Evidence history stored in `Extra["events"]` does not affect queue derivation.
196	- Claim metadata does not affect queue derivation beyond the bead's status.
197	
198	## Concurrency Model
199	
200	- Directory locks prevent simultaneous writes.
201	- Reads are allowed without the lock, but the repair path uses the lock before
202	  rewriting anything.
203	- No API writes directly to `beads.jsonl`; all mutation goes through `WriteAll`
204	  or a higher-level store method that calls it.
205	- External tools are expected to use `ddx bead`, not edit the JSONL directly.
206	
207	This model is sufficient for the expected HELIX/DDX usage pattern:
208	multiple agents can race to claim or update work, but the store will serialize
209	the resulting file writes and either preserve or repair the tracker snapshot.
210	
211	## Performance Targets
212	
213	The feature spec sets the target at under 100 ms for local operations on up to
214	10,000 beads. The design supports that target by:
215	
216	- using a single sequential parse of the JSONL file
217	- avoiding database overhead
218	- deriving queue views from one snapshot
219	- keeping writes as a single temp-file pass plus rename
220	
221	## Validation Matrix
222	
223	These are the key tests that validate the design:
224	
225	- `go test ./internal/bead/...`
226	- `go test ./cmd -run 'TestBead' -count=1`
227	- `TestConcurrentCreatesSerialized`
228	- `TestMalformedJSONLSkipsBadRecords`
229	- `TestMalformedJSONLAllBadReturnsError`
230	- `TestBeadClaimUsesExplicitAssignee`
231	- `TestBeadClaimFallsBackToCallerIdentity`
232	- `TestBeadUnclaimClearsClaimMetadata`
233	- `TestBeadEvidenceAppendPreservesOrder`
234	- `TestBeadEvidenceAppendAtomicWithConcurrentWriters`
235	- `TestBeadShowJSONIncludesEvidenceHistory`
236	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`
237	
238	Expected outcomes:
239	
240	- Concurrent creates complete without corruption.
241	- A mixed-validity JSONL file is repaired with a `.bak` backup.
242	- An all-malformed JSONL file fails with a contextual error.
243	- Queue commands keep working when at least one valid record remains.
244	
245	## Failure Modes
246	
247	- If the file is completely malformed, the command fails instead of fabricating
248	  empty state.
249	- If the repair backup cannot be written, the read returns an error and leaves
250	  the original file untouched.
251	- If a lock cannot be acquired in time, the mutating command fails cleanly.
252	- If evidence append fails, the mutation fails atomically and the prior bead
253	  snapshot remains unchanged.
254	
255	## Non-Goals
256	
257	- Real-time sync between multiple bead stores.
258	- Database-backed mutation logic in the default backend.
259	- Workflow-specific validation policy in the bead engine itself.
260	
261	Those concerns belong in higher layers such as HELIX hooks, import/export, or
262	alternative backends.
263

129:The primary work queue is one collection, not the only possible collection.
131:- The default active-work collection maps to `beads.jsonl` in the JSONL backend.
132:- Other DDx services may use separate bead-backed collections for archived
136:  primary collection row.

input.content:
---
ddx:
  id: TD-027
  depends_on:
    - ADR-004
    - SD-004
    - FEAT-004
---
# Technical Design: Bead-Backed Collection Abstraction

## Purpose

This design picks the concrete implementation choices needed to land
ADR-004 ("Use Bead-Backed Collections for DDx Runtime Storage") on top
of the existing single-file `.ddx/beads.jsonl` tracker described in
SD-004 and required by FEAT-004.

ADR-004 commits DDx to a model of named bead-backed collections with
sidecar attachments. SD-004 describes the existing single-file store and
its locking, repair, and append-only evidence semantics. FEAT-004 sets
the user-visible behavior of the active tracker. This TD specifies:

(a) the collection registry shape,
(b) the archival trigger policy and parameters,
(c) the attachment storage layout,
(d) the migration semantics for the existing 5.4 MB `beads.jsonl`,
(e) the read-path semantics across active plus archive, and
(f) the bd/br interchange compatibility for the archive collection.

The decisions below are the defaults DDx will ship. Anything outside
this list — for example new collections beyond `beads`, `beads-archive`,
`exec-runs`, and `agent-sessions` — is out of scope for this TD and
should be handled in a follow-up TD that depends on this one.

## (a) Collection Registry

### Registry Shape

A collection is a named logical store backed by one of the existing
bead backends. The registry is an in-process Go map seeded at startup
from a small declarative table. It is not a user-editable file; users
do not name new collections, and DDx does not auto-discover collections
from disk.

```go
type CollectionID string

type CollectionSpec struct {
    ID              CollectionID // "beads", "beads-archive", ...
    DefaultBackend  BackendKind  // jsonl | bd | br
    JSONLPath       string       // path under .ddx/ for the jsonl backend
    QueueSemantics  bool         // true for "beads" only
    ArchivePartner  CollectionID // "beads-archive" for "beads", "" otherwise
    Attachments     bool         // true for collections that allow sidecars
}
```

Concrete shipping registry:

| ID               | JSONL path                       | QueueSemantics | ArchivePartner | Attachments |
|------------------|----------------------------------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| `beads`          | `.ddx/beads.jsonl`               | yes            | `beads-archive`| no          |
| `beads-archive`  | `.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl`       | no             | (none)         | no          |
| `exec-runs`      | `.ddx/exec-runs.jsonl`           | no             | (none)         | yes         |
| `agent-sessions` | `.ddx/agent-sessions.jsonl`      | no             | (none)         | yes         |

### Store API

The current `beads.Store` API is generalized so the file path is no
longer hardcoded. The minimum surface is:

```go
type Store interface {
    Collection(id CollectionID) Collection
}

type Collection interface {
    List(ctx context.Context) ([]Bead, error)
    Get(ctx context.Context, id string) (Bead, error)
    Append(ctx context.Context, b Bead) error
    Update(ctx context.Context, b Bead) error
    Delete(ctx context.Context, id string) error
    Lock(ctx context.Context) (Unlocker, error)
}
```

`Lock` is per-collection so that archiving from `beads` to
`beads-archive` can hold both locks in a fixed order (`beads` first,
then `beads-archive`) to avoid deadlocks.

The existing locking, atomic temp-file rename, malformed-record repair,
and `Extra` preservation behaviors from SD-004 carry over unchanged on
a per-collection basis. Each JSONL collection has its own `.lock`,
`.tmp`, and `.bak` files alongside its primary file.

### Backend Selection

`bd` and `br` backends already accept a collection name through their
own CLIs. The DDx external-backend adapter passes the collection ID
through to those CLIs. For backends that do not support multiple named
collections, the adapter returns a clear error when a non-default
collection is requested rather than silently merging records.

## (b) Archival Trigger Policy

### Goal

Keep the active queue small enough that `ddx bead list` and the SD-004
read path stay under the 100 ms / 10,000 bead target stated in SD-004,
without forcing operators to think about archival.

### Trigger

Archival runs on demand through `ddx bead archive` and opportunistically
after any close-causing mutation when the active collection exceeds a
threshold. There is no background daemon.

### Defaults

| Parameter                      | Default                                |
|--------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| `archive.enabled`              | `true`                                 |
| `archive.min_age`              | `30d` since `closed_at`                |
| `archive.min_active_count`     | `2000` records in `beads`              |
| `archive.batch_size`           | `500` per opportunistic pass           |
| `archive.statuses`             | `closed`, `wont_fix`, `superseded`     |
| `archive.opportunistic`        | `true` (run after close mutations)     |
| `archive.preserve_dependencies`| `true` (keep edges resolvable)         |

A bead is eligible to archive when its `status` is in
`archive.statuses`, its `closed_at` (or `updated_at` fallback) is older
than `archive.min_age`, and no open bead in `beads` lists it as a
dependency. The dependency check prevents archiving a closed bead that
an open bead still references; archived beads remain readable, but
queue derivation in SD-004 should not have to load `beads-archive` to
decide `ready`/`blocked`.

### Configuration

Defaults live in `config.go` and may be overridden through `.ddx.yml`:

```yaml
archive:
  enabled: true
  min_age: 30d
  min_active_count: 2000
  batch_size: 500
  statuses: [closed, wont_fix, superseded]
  opportunistic: true
```

### Mutation Sequence

1. Acquire `beads` lock.
2. Acquire `beads-archive` lock.
3. Read both snapshots.
4. Select up to `batch_size` eligible records from `beads`.
5. Append the selected records to `beads-archive` with an added
   `archived_at` timestamp in `Extra`.
6. Remove the selected records from `beads`.
7. Atomic temp-file rename for `beads-archive` first, then for `beads`.
   This ordering means a crash mid-archive leaves a record in both
   collections rather than nowhere, and the read path in (e) hides the
   duplicate behind active-wins precedence.
8. Release locks in reverse order.

## (c) Attachment Storage Layout

### Layout

Attachments are sidecar files under a per-collection directory keyed by
record ID:

```
.ddx/attachments/
  <collection-id>/
    <record-id>/
      events.jsonl
      prompt.txt
      response.txt
      stdout.log
      <name>.<ext>
```

Concrete defaults:

- The active `beads` collection does not use sidecar attachments.
  Evidence stays in `Extra["events"]` per SD-004, because that history
  is small and is referenced often by queue tooling.
- `exec-runs` and `agent-sessions` use sidecars by default for prompt,
  response, stdout, stderr, and structured result blobs.
- `beads-archive` does not introduce new attachments. Records moved
  from `beads` carry their existing `Extra["events"]` history inline.

### Reference Format

Attachment references are stored under a reserved
`Extra["attachments"]` array on the record:

```json
{
  "attachments": [
    {
      "name": "prompt",
      "path": "exec-runs/run-2026-05-01-abc/prompt.txt",
      "media_type": "text/plain",
      "sha256": "…",
      "size": 12345
    }
  ]
}
```

`path` is repository-relative under `.ddx/attachments/`. `sha256` and
`size` are recorded at write time and verified on read. The reference
key `attachments` is DDx-specific and lives in preserved extras as
required by ADR-004; it does not rename or shadow any bd/br field.

### Write Algorithm

1. Write the attachment to a temp path under
   `.ddx/attachments/<collection>/<id>/.<name>.tmp`.
2. `fsync`, then rename to the final path.
3. Compute and record `sha256` and `size`.
4. Append the reference to `Extra["attachments"]`.
5. Persist the record through the normal collection write path so the
   reference becomes durable atomically with the temp-file rename of
   the collection JSONL file.

### Garbage Collection

Attachments are removed only when their owning record is removed. A
`ddx bead gc` command (out of scope for this TD beyond the contract)
walks `.ddx/attachments/<collection>/` and deletes directories whose
record IDs no longer exist in the collection. There is no time-based
attachment expiration.

## (d) Migration Plan for Existing `beads.jsonl`

The current `.ddx/beads.jsonl` is 5.4 MB and 1,172 lines. The
migration must be safe to run on an in-place repository checkout.

### Steps

1. **Compatibility shim first.** The new collection registry treats
   `.ddx/beads.jsonl` as the JSONL path for the `beads` collection. No
   file move is required to keep the active tracker working. Existing
   readers and writers continue to operate against the same path.

2. **Idempotent create-on-write for archive.** The
   `.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl` file is created lazily on the first
   archive operation. Absence of the file is treated as an empty
   archive collection.

3. **One-shot backfill command.** `ddx bead migrate-archive` performs
   the initial archival pass:
   - Acquire both locks.
   - Make a backup copy of `.ddx/beads.jsonl` to
     `.ddx/beads.jsonl.pre-archive.bak`.
   - Move all records that match the archival policy in (b) using the
     normal mutation sequence.
   - Print a summary of moved/retained counts.
   This command is opt-in. Users who never run it keep the current
   single-file behavior.

4. **No schema rewrite.** Existing records are not rewritten to add
   new fields. The `attachments` extra appears only on new
   `exec-runs` and `agent-sessions` records.

5. **Rollback.** `ddx bead migrate-archive --rollback` re-merges
   `beads-archive.jsonl` into `beads.jsonl` under both locks, atomic
   temp-file rename, and removes the archive file when empty.

6. **Doc audit.** The migration command is documented in the bead
   command reference. `ddx doc audit` should pass after this TD lands
   because the new TD declares its dependencies and no existing artifact
   needs to change its `depends_on`.

### Risk Mitigation

- The default `archive.min_active_count` of 2000 means existing
  installations under that count will not be archived opportunistically
  on close. They keep behaving like today's single-file tracker until
  they cross the threshold or run `ddx bead migrate-archive`.
- The pre-archive backup file is never removed automatically.

## (e) Read-Path Semantics Across Active and Archive

### Default View

Queue commands defined by SD-004 (`list`, `ready`, `blocked`, `status`)
read only the `beads` collection. They do not load `beads-archive`.
This preserves the SD-004 100 ms target as the active set stays bounded
and matches user expectation that "what's on the queue" is the active
queue.

### Merged View

`ddx bead show <id>`, `ddx bead history`, and any explicit
`--include-archive` flag use a merged view:

1. Look up the bead in `beads` first.
2. Fall back to `beads-archive` if not found.
3. For listing operations with `--include-archive`, lazily concatenate
   the two snapshots and de-duplicate by `id` with active-wins
   precedence (so a record present in both collections after an
   interrupted archive is reported as the active copy).

### Lazy Loading

The archive collection is opened only when a merged view is requested.
The store keeps per-collection snapshots cached for the lifetime of a
single command invocation; commands do not share snapshots across
invocations.

### Deletion Semantics

`ddx bead delete <id>` removes from the collection that currently holds
the record. If the record is in both collections (post-crash), the
delete removes it from both under both locks before returning success.

### Dependency Resolution

When the queue derivation in SD-004 needs to display a closed
dependency that has been archived, it reads only the dependency's
`status` from `beads-archive` on demand and caches it in memory for the
remainder of the command. It never promotes archived beads back into
the active snapshot.

## (f) bd/br Interchange Compatibility for the Archive Collection

### Constraints from ADR-004

ADR-004 requires that bd/br interchange continue to work through
`list --json` for reads and `import --from jsonl --replace -` for
writes, and that the bead envelope keeps its shared field names.

### Decision

The archive collection participates in interchange on the same terms
as the active collection.

- `ddx bead export --collection beads-archive` emits one bead-record
  JSON object per line, identical schema to active beads.
- `ddx bead import --collection beads-archive --from jsonl -` accepts
  the same shape.
- `archived_at` is stored in `Extra` and round-trips as an unknown
  field for bd/br, exactly like other DDx-specific fields. It is not
  promoted to a top-level field.
- The `attachments` extra also round-trips. bd/br do not interpret it,
  but they do not strip preserved extras, so references survive an
  export/import cycle.

### Adapter Behavior

For backends that support multiple named collections, the DDx adapter
passes the collection ID through. For backends that do not, the
adapter returns an error when the user asks for a non-default
collection rather than silently merging records into the default
collection. This is the same rule as (a).

### Schema Compatibility Test

`schema_compat_test.go` is extended with an "archive round-trip" case:
take a record from the active collection, archive it, export it,
re-import it into a fresh `beads-archive` collection, and verify
field-for-field equality including preserved extras.

## Validation

These checks must pass after this TD is implemented:

- `go test ./internal/bead/...`
- `go test ./cmd -run 'TestBeadCollection|TestArchive|TestAttachment' -count=1`
- `ddx doc audit` shows TD-027 with no broken edges.
- Manual: run `ddx bead migrate-archive` on a copy of an existing
  repository checkout and confirm queue commands produce the same
  active snapshot they did before migration.

## Non-Goals

- Adding collections beyond `beads`, `beads-archive`, `exec-runs`, and
  `agent-sessions`. Future collections require a follow-up TD.
- Real-time replication between collections.
- Cross-repository archive sharing.
- Background or scheduled archival; archival only runs on user-initiated
  commands or as an opportunistic step inside an already-mutating
  command.

TD-027 covers all six required sections (a)–(f) with concrete defaults (registry shape with shipping table, archival trigger 30d/2000-count/500-batch, attachment layout `.ddx/attachments/<collection>/<id>/`, migration via `ddx bead migrate-archive` with backup, active-only default reads with merged-view on demand, and bd/br interchange via export/import with collection flag).
